tlos: TLO 1. Recall the basics of MATLAB including declaring variables, simple arithmetic operations, vectors and arrays, loops, functions, and plotting.

events.description: This event completed prior to arrival at the school gives the student the necessary skills using MATLAB for the following modules within the course.

tlos: TLO 1. Use the basic vocabulary of orbits to describe a satellite's motion through space.
TLO 2. Transform satellite position between a variety of inertial and relative coordinate frames.
TLO 3. Propagate orbits over time to estimate the future position of a satellite.
TLO 4. Complete multi-step calculations to complete an orbit transfer in inertial space and assess the pros and cons of different typed of transfer maneuvers.
TLO 5. Understand the maneuver primitives associated with satellite motion in relative space.

events.description: This block includes the definition of space, basic physics of space, the natural space environment, the man-made space environment, the physical laws that govern the movement of an orbital body, Kepler's Laws, Newton's Laws, Universal Laws of Gravity, and movement in orbits, solar winds and other effects on space craft. The lecture is followed by a series of case studies of issues/failures of satellites encountered due to the Space Environment related to the environment in practical situations.

The lecture is followed by a series of case studies of issues/failures of satellites encountered due to the Space Environment related to the environment in practical situations.

These lectures cover Inertial Orbits with topics in Orbit Basics, Classical Orbital Elements, Orbit Regimes, Sidereal & Solar Time Comparison, Perturbations, Maneuver Forensics as related to Perturbations, Ground Traces, Orbital Coordinate Frames, and Time Systems. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented, create their own tools to perform repeated calculations, and will be assigned homework and projects to reinforce concepts presented in block. Students will utilize Systems Tool Kit Software & www.usspacetraining.com as learning aids in visualizing concepts and for use in assigned projects.

events.description: These blocks cover Inertial Maneuvers with topics including Orbit Velocity, Hohmann Transfer, Plane Changes, Combined Maneuvers, Launch & Transfer Maneuvers to GEO orbits, Station-keeping, Re-location, Lambert Transfers, and Mission aspects of orbit maneuvering. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented, build on their own tools to perform repeated calculations, and will be assigned homework and projects to reinforce concepts presented in block. Students will utilize excel tools, Systems Tool Kit Software & www.usspacetraining.com as aids in visualizing concepts and for use in assigned projects

tlos: TLO 1. Use the basic vocabulary of orbits to describe a satellite's motion through space.
TLO 2. Transform satellite position between a variety of inertial and relative coordinate frames.
TLO 3. Describe Kalmann filters and use them in Space Domain Awareness (SDA) applications.
TLO 4. Describe the components of an attitude control subsystem and explain their interactions.
TLO 5. Calculate satellite attitude using quaternions.

events.description: This section covers Initial Orbit Determination with topics including Orbit Determination approaches, Initial Orbit Determination and Batch processing. Students will utilize excel tools and Systems Tool Kit Software & www.usspacetraining.com as aids in visualizing concepts. Students will execute an orbital determination project using MATLAB. A test covering the Inertial Orbit Material will be administered to confirm comprehension of the material.

This exercise is the integration event for Estimation and Kalman Filtering. Students will be given a scenario to evaluate the relative navigation system with multiple Kalman filter settings for a new satellite capable of rendezvous and proximity operations. Students will be given a test plan and measures to execute within the WebTools simulator and evaluate their system using a series of pre-planned test maneuvers. Students must apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on test results. After executing a series of test points, the test team will have to mission plan a new test point to utilize the remaining available velocity of the satellite within the simulator to get a more complete data set on one of the Kalman filters. Upon completion of their test points, students will complete and turn in a limited scope report based on the test data.

Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented and will be assigned homework to reinforce concepts presented in block.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand the use of different control methods and their applications.
TLO 2. Understand the use of different simulations and their applications.
TLO 3. Understand different data collection methods and their applications.
TLO 4. Evaluate the "sim to real gap" for a given scenario.
TLO 5. Create hazard and risk mitigation strategies for various autonomy scenarios.
TLO 6. Apply control methods to different applications.
TLO 7. Analyze robustness methods and training tools for their accuracy and proper information.
TLO 8. Analyze methods for testing autonomous systems.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences Lecture
events.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It includes a broad scope of topics that build an understanding of aspects to consider when testing an autonomy-based system. Topics include identifying and analyzing different types of control systems and when to apply them, describing different categories of data and proper methods for their analysis, describing different forms and fidelity of simulation and their application, analyzing the use of Monte Carlo testing to include its advantages and pitfalls, and identifying key issues to consider when building up to and executing flight tests. It is taught consecutively with FQ 7300 Data-Driven Control.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences Exam
events.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It is taught consecutively with FQ 7300 Data-Driven Control.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe the overall function and flow of a satellite propulsion system and calculate thrust using the rocket equation.

TLO 2. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to describe how control systems are used in relative flight and manipulate controller settings to optimize performance based on mission type.

TLO 3. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to describe the components of an attitude control subsystem and explain their interactions.
TLO 4. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to calculate satellite attitude using quaternions.

TLO 5. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to describe the components of an attitude control subsystem and explain their interactions.
TLO 6. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to calculate satellite attitude using quaternions.

TLO 7. Describe how control systems are used in relative flight and use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to manipulate controller settings to optimize performance based on mission type
TLO 8. Use data driven test and AI to analyze and evaluate test data to formulate conclusions and recommendations to calculate satellite attitude using quaternions.
TLO 9. Evaluate the mass properties of the System Under Test
TLO 10. Evaluate the power generation and distribution system as it is defined within the designed reference orbit
TLO 11. Evaluate the thermal management system of the system under test within the designed reference orbit
TLO 12. Construct a model for Orbital Average Power and a Designed Reference Orbit Thermal Budget
TLO 13. Provide an overview of the assembly, integration, characterization of subsystems, and mission testing conducted prior to launch.
TLO 14. Report characterization, evaluation, and recommendations of lab events in a technical test report deliverable for each lab session.

events.description: These blocks review common spacecraft systems, the systems engineering process that influences design decisions and design budgets. An overview of each major subsystem, EPS, Structure, Command and Data Handling System, thermal management and Attitude Determination and Control System. Students will be introduced to the EyasSat laboratory training model in preparation for labs.

Students will be introduced to the EyasSat laboratory training model in preparation for labs.

events.eventName: Satellite Attitude Dynamics, Estimation, and Control Lecture
events.description: These blocks elaborate on nonlinear (and linearized) motion state dynamics (including autogenetic gravity center location); nonlinear state estimation; and nonlinear and linear state control (e.g. disturbance rejection). Lectures include time propagation of Euler's coupled, nonlinear moment equations relative to inertial reference frames and expressed in coordinates of the body reference frame. Luenberger observers are introduced as one option for nonlinear state estimation. Nonlinear feedforward control is introduced with dynamic inversion for feedback, optionally augmented by linear state feedback control. Structural dynamics are taught in the context of controlling highly flexible space robotics with hands-on elaboration on the robotic on-orbit trainer during AS 7140S GROOT Handling Qualities. These lectures can take place before or after AS 7150O. The lab provides additional details into attitude estimation and control design considerations, reference frames, Euler angles & quaternions, coordinate frame rotations, attitude sensor types / design, and an introduction to attitude control. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented and create their own tools to perform repeated calculations. This lab provides hands-on experience using the Aerocube satellite system of the material learned in AS 7110A Satellite Attitude Estimation & Control.

This lecture provides additional details into Power Subsystem architecture, details on solar panels, battery design, power distribution, conditioning & fusing, fault design, and power balance analysis. Students will work on a project with a provided excel tool for determining operations design to ensure positive energy balance for a CubeSat.

Students will conduct multiple lab exercises using the EyasSat small satellite model aligned to the various satellite subsystems including the EPS, Structure, GNC and CDH. Basic tests will be conducted to familiarize students with the subsystems and determine if the EyasSat meets specified requirements.

Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite in a variety of configurations and analyze the platform's stability in translational and rotational maneuver. Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite including ability to perform the mission it was designed for. This exercise will require students to apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on their test results.
Students will plan a series of passes to assess the Aerocube ADCS. Students will use their test results to develop an updated moment of inertia matrix for the satellite.

Students will learn loads analysis for single degree of freedom systems; attitude trajectories; issues we create; deterministic artificial intelligence; exact linear parameterization vs. linearization; mass moments of inertia (matrix/tensor); feedback control and observers: and linear and nonlinear motion state observers. A 2-body mini-Groot example will be offered leading to understandings of structural filtering and culminating in a computation lab assignment: an n-body mini-Groot control with Bandpass + Notch and trajectory shaping. The culminating hands-on activity includes application to GROOT.

events.description: This block provides an overview of typical initial satellite testing after launch, with examples of failures, setting priorities for test to minimize probability of failure, insertion orbit considerations, timelines and challenges to timelines like space weather.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe the overall function and flow of a satellite propulsion system and calculate thrust using the rocket equation.
TLO 2. Describe the components of an attitude control subsystem and explain their interactions.

events.description: This lecture describes propulsion basics, including the thrust equation, specific impulse and efficiency, the basic properties of a rocket nozzle, solid fueled rockets, liquid propellant rockets, advantages and disadvantages of different fuels, and briefly discusses some rocket testing requirements. Additionally, on-orbit propulsion systems, including function, limitations, and applications will be discussed. Concepts are reinforced with a tour of the AFRL rocket test facilities at Edwards AFB. Students will develop background knowledge to perform basic calculations of rocket/thruster performance.

Students will develop background knowledge to perform basic calculations of rocket/thruster performance.

Demonstrate and practice test planning, conduct and reporting for V&V of a rocket trajectory simulation. Students will evaluate a simulation developed by another student group through ground and open-air test using representative model rockets. Students will assemble model rockets and integrate instrumentation systems. Students will develop a test plan, execute test event(s) and report on results.

Students will utilize excel tools and www.usspacetraining.com. Projects will include using excel tools to determine appropriate controller settings and then the simulator to introduce how controllers are used in flight missions. A test or larger project will be administered covering the last three topics on Relative Orbits to assess student comprehension.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand how measurement type and uncertainty affect the accuracy of our state estimate and how we characterize the "goodness" of our estimate.
TLO 2. Understand the use of Kalman filters to estimate the relative state of a vehicle performing an RPO.

events.eventName: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation Lecture
events.description: This course will teach students advanced Kalman Filtering and relative navigation for space applications. Students will develop an understanding between sensor performance measures and Kalman filter performance with appropriate test measures. The students will also gain an understanding of how RPO profiles and maneuvers affect state estimates and ability to assess a Rel Nav system. Students will gain hands on experience with the simulator on www.usspacetraining.com to demonstrate key concepts and prepare them for estimation testing and the subsequent estimation exercise

events.eventName: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation Assessment
events.description: This course will teach students advanced Kalman Filtering and relative navigation for space applications. Students will develop an understanding between sensor performance measures and Kalman filter performance with appropriate test measures. The students will also gain an understanding of how RPO profiles and maneuvers affect state estimates and ability to assess a Rel Nav system. Students will gain hands on experience with the simulator on www.usspacetraining.com to demonstrate key concepts and prepare them for estimation testing and the subsequent estimation exercise

events.eventName: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation Debrief
events.description: This course will teach students advanced Kalman Filtering and relative navigation for space applications. Students will develop an understanding between sensor performance measures and Kalman filter performance with appropriate test measures. The students will also gain an understanding of how RPO profiles and maneuvers affect state estimates and ability to assess a Rel Nav system. Students will gain hands on experience with the simulator on www.usspacetraining.com to demonstrate key concepts and prepare them for estimation testing and the subsequent estimation exercise

The students will be given a test plan and measures to execute within the Webtools simulator. Students will evaluate their system in the simulator using a series of pre-planned test maneuvers. This exercise will also require students to apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on their test results. After executing a series of test points, the test team will have to mission plan a new test point to utilize the remaining delta-v on the satellite within the simulator to get a more complete data set on one of the Kalman filters. Upon completion of their test points, students will complete and turn in a limited scope report based on the test data. This exercise is the integration event for Estimation and Kalman filtering. Students will be given a scenario to evaluate the relative navigation system w/multiple Kalman filter settings for a new RPO-capable satellite.

prerequisites.name: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation Lecture

tlos: TLO 1. Perform a threat surface analysis for a satellite system.
TLO 2. Develop a campaign to test countermeasures, and tactics, techniques and procedures (TTP) designed to mitigate against a threat.

events.eventName: Advanced Topics in Astronautical Sciences Lecture
events.description: Directed advanced study in Astronautical Engineering on a subject of mutual interest to student and staff member. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.
Individual study and research supervised by a faculty/staff member. Topic established with the department head. Final report.

events.eventName: Independent Study in Astronautical Sciences Report
events.description: Individual study and research supervised by a faculty/staff member. Topic established with the department head. Final report.

This course is intended to provide students with a foundational understanding of the unique aerodynamic principles, thermal challenges, and vehicle dynamics associated with atmospheric re-entry from orbit.

events.eventName: Re-entry Navigation and Low L/D Flight
events.description: This course is intended to provide an understanding of the specific GNC challenges of guiding a low-maneuverability vehicle to a precise atmospheric interface point.
This course is intended to equip students with a comprehensive understanding of the space environment, threats, and the systems used to maintain awareness.

tlos: TLO 1. Recall critical concepts of space threats.

events.description: Lectures cover the five major threat threads of Co-orbital Anti-Satellites (ASAT), direct ascent ASAT, Directed Energy Weapons (DEW), Cyber Attack, and Ground Infrastructure Attack. This course will consist of a series of classified lectures and will discuss adversary threats with the potential to restrict US freedom of action in space and disrupt, deny, degrade, and destroy space assets providing critical capabilities to the warfighter. Synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

This course will consist of a series of classified lectures and will discuss adversary threats with the potential to restrict US freedom of action in space and disrupt, deny, degrade, and destroy space assets providing critical capabilities to the warfighter. Synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

This workshop is the synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain how the WSEP is applied to Space Programs.

events.eventName: Weapons System Evaluation Program (WSEP) Guest Lecture
events.description: This lesson will be taught at the TS/SCI level. A guest lecturer will discuss the Weapons System Evaluation Program as it relates to space programs. The lesson will discuss the creation and development of the program, and some of the challenges and successes.

tlos: TLO 1. Discuss information from classified programs and describe how lessons learned relate to inertial and relative orbit courseware.

events.description: These lectures will cover Classified Topics with a session at the conclusion of the Inertial Orbit topics and a session at the end of the Relative Orbit topics. Students will be able to share their experiences from prior classified programs and will be introduced to several topics including information on our recent adversary activities in space, and lessons learned from recent US activities.

tlos: TLO 1. Recall critical information passed on from various guest lectures.

events.eventName: Space Shuttle Proximity Maneuvering - Guest Lecture
events.description: This lecture will be taught in an unclassified setting as a guest lecture and will provide a description of the rendezvous process and issues associated with close proximity maneuvering with another orbiting object. This includes the description of the space shuttle aircraft and MIR, the role of the space shuttle pilot in rendezvous and close proximity maneuvering, and provides essential lessons learned from extended close proximity operations.
